Listen to my voice, mortal.

I am Hastur, the King in Yellow.

Tomorrow, when I descend, I shall appoint you as the leader of the Old Ones, to lead their minions, and grant you eternal life!

Some deceive, others grant gifts.

Believe me.

So starting from the foundations of their relationship: the exchange
- horla and colette are childhood friends, and together are renting out the garden of a noblewoman’s house. Horla lives in the greenhouse primarily, since she’s shy/overwhelmed by people, and Colette sells roses from the garden to continue supporting them
These are the facts, nothing toxic here, it’s all healthy. At this duration, we get the line in the pv, “I will give you a garden. In our kingdom, I will bear all the thorns for you. I am the garden, and you are Spring”
This line is essentially what DEFINES the horla/colette relationship for this first period of time, with Horla writing her poems in the greenhouse, and Colette dreaming of greater things.
Phase 2: dreams
- as they get older and time passes, they realize their dreams/life goals/ambitions don’t align. While Colette yearned for more, wanting to one day own the noblewoman’s house/a grand house, Horla was content with the world they had then, but the issue starts here. Colette fundamentally CANNOT understand why Horla doesn’t want more, since she thinks it’s natural for people to be driven by ambition. In addition, there’s always been a disconnect and comparison between them in Colette’s eyes, since Horla was the “perfect girl” people would want: mild-mannered, quiet, not loud, not opinionated, not Colette. But she had her role as their provider, and they FIT into those roles, which comforted Colette in a way. 
P3: However, when Horla started seeing success, she began to feel inferior to her, and that eventually festers over to the Fire and Horla’s death
- horla isn’t even mad over it btw!! When asked about it ingame she gets sad and is more regretful that she wrote the poems instead

So Colette, blinded by her inferiority complex + jealousy towards Horla, traps her in the greenhouse fire, and we have those events go down. Following that, we reach the actual contents of the spec ops, where Keeper comes in

Enter Cité and the World of Poetry
- so despite being so jealous of Horla and all this, we know that Colette *does* regret everything in the end, as evidenced by the last few lines before her death. Horla forgives her, which is the turning point for me, and the thing is, she DIDN’T HAVE TO. Horla, despite all the events that go down in the spec ops: searching the city, colette’s claiming of horla’s work as her own, she is STILL forgiven. By Horla, who feels emotions so strongly that they bleed through to the world around her post-awakening. The moment of Horla’s forgiveness is also arguably the moment of her finally overcoming the circumstances around her death, as I view it from the CGs of her breaking out through the thorns to caress Colette because that is A Moment
Moving on to more theory stuff about them:
- colette kept and shared Horla’s poems because she still Cared after her death, and wanted the world to see the poetry/art Horla made, but chose to do it under her own name because deep down she still felt abandoned by Horla/still felt a little jealous inside
- iirc in the story Horla specifically states that she will never forget what happened, which I take as she’ll always CHOOSE to remember Colette post-mortem, despite everything that’s happened because she still cares about Colette, all the parts of her, even the good & bad ones
Another thing is, Colette DID care for Horla, and their previous shared dream!! She gave up an amulet/locket her mother had left her in ORDER for them to rent out the garden, she did it as a surprise FOR horla, knowing that Horla had always loved the flowers there!!
Also even if BIAV can’t outright say they were girlfriends because censorship there is legitimately no other way to explain this HELLO????

And the worst part is it goes both ways because for so long both of them were content with Horla just staying in the greenhouse to just take care of herself and write what she wanted all while Colette worked on so many things to keep them together so while Colette sacrificed so much, Horla really didn’t and that was also a big factor
